title: Flawed Merge Sort on Doubly Linked List

statement.inputFormat: A doubly linked list is represented as a list of nodes, where each node has a value, a previous node, and a next node.
statement.outputFormat: The `head` of the merged linked list.
statement.notes: You can only access the linked list using the `head` node.

hints.text: Think about how to merge two sorted linked lists correctly.
Look at how the `prev` pointers are being handled in the buggy implementation.

editorial.title: Editorial: Flawed Merge Sort on Doubly Linked List
editorial.intuition: The `merge` function should iterate through the two linked lists simultaneously, comparing the values of the nodes and merging them accordingly.
editorial.bruteForce: The buggy implementation has a time complexity of O(n + n) = O(n) and a space complexity of O(n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(1), assuming we don't count the extra space needed for recursion.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly handling the `prev` pointers in the linked list.
Not considering edge cases such as empty linked lists.

solutions.python:
import sys
from typing import List

class Node:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val
        self.prev = None
        self.next = None

class Solution:
    def read(self):
        n = int(input())
        nodes = list(map(int, input().split()))
        head = Node(nodes[0])
        curr = head
        for i in range(1, n):
            curr.next = Node(nodes[i])
            curr.next.prev = curr
            curr = curr.next
        return head

    def merge(self, l1, l2):
        dummy = Node(0)
        curr = dummy
        while l1 and l2:
            if l1.val < l2.val:
                curr.next = l1
                l1.prev = curr
                curr = curr.next
                l1 = l1.next
            else:
                curr.next = l2
                l2.prev = curr
                curr = curr.next
                l2 = l2.next
        if l1:
            curr.next = l1
            l1.prev = curr
        else:
            curr.next = l2
        while curr.next:
            curr = curr.next
        return dummy.next

    def print(self, head):
        while head:
            print(head.val, end=' ')
            head = head.next
        print()

t = Solution()
t.head = t.read()
l1 = t.head
llist_size = 0
n = int(input())
l2 = t.read()
l1 = t.merge(l1, l2)
t.print(l1)
